title: Where indigenous water management practices overcome failures of structures: The Wadi Laba spate irrigation system in Eritrea
adaptation_focus: Traditional/indigenous water management practices, full community labor participation, rotational water-sharing rules, and coordinated seasonal migration to upper catchments to manage unpredictable flood events and structural failures in spate irrigation
indicators_measured: Irrigated surface area, flood discharge estimation ($m^3s^{-1}$) mapped over time, loss of irrigable land due to erosion/sand intrusion (hectares), vegetation cover reduction (%), walking distance to fetch brushwood, and conflict occurrences
strengths_and_limitations: The study benefits from prolonged field presence providing velocity-area flood data paired directly with historical community interviews;Structural modernization was planned and initiated with very limited baseline data on traditional operations; groundwater resources in the area remain systematically unstudied
lessons_learned: The "full farmers' participation" principle creates a mandatory community obligation where all farmers contribute labor to repair damaged infrastructure, ensuring the system can withstand devastating high-magnitude floods. This collective effort is reinforced by unique, equitable water-sharing rules—such as giving absolute priority rights to tail-end farmers during large floods and prioritizing fields left dry from previous seasons—which foster the widespread community trust and cooperation necessary to sustain physical structures. Furthermore, the community balances its local maintenance duties with seasonal migration to the upper catchment for supplementary food and fodder by enforcing strict regulations: critical assets like draught oxen must remain on-site, and sub-group and group leaders must operate on alternating rotational shifts to ensure continuous system oversight. Ultimately, as technical modernization projects are implemented, a high degree of discrepancy between new structural designs and these deeply embedded indigenous water management arrangements could risk the long-term viability and success of the development efforts.

title: Crop-climate model in support of adjusting local ecological calendar in the Taxkorgan, eastern Pamir Plateau
adaptation_focus: Adjusting local ecological/crop calendars (sowing and harvesting dates) based on thermal accumulation (GDD) and water requirements (ETc) to mitigate climate change risks
indicators_measured: Growing degree days (GDD), crop evapotranspiration (ETc), crop yield/production, onset dates of green-up (BGS), onset dates of dormancy (EGS), length of growing season (LGS)
strengths_and_limitations: The assessment benefits from integrating historical climatic records, remote sensing phenology metrics, and local Indigenous ethnoecological knowledge. However, a limitation noted is that barley production and plantation area data were not continuous over the timeline, restricting full empirical evaluation for that specific crop.
lessons_learned: Warming in high-altitude mountain regions shortens the number of days required for crop maturation, which acts as a benefit that progressively increases crop yields if farming practices adapt.

title: Managing Environmental Risk in Presence of Climate Change: The Role of Adaptation in the Nile Basin of Ethiopia
adaptation_focus: Portfolio of strategies including changing crop varieties, adopting water harvesting, and implementing soil and water conservation.
process_outcome_domains: decision_making_planning, uptake_adoption, participation_coproduction , yields_productivity, wellbeing, risk_reduction, resilience_adaptive_capacity
indicators_measured: Downside risk exposure measured by the skewness of crop yields (the third central moment of the crop yield distribution); agricultural productivity / quantity produced per hectare
strengths_and_limitations: Controls for unobserved heterogeneity, sample selection bias, and farmer skills using an endogenous switching regression model combined with Mundlak’s fixed effects approach. Uses a flexible moment-based specification to measure actual downside risk (crop failure) rather than just variance;Relies strictly on cross-sectional data which prevents analyzing dynamic aspects of risk management decisions over time. Does not distinguish or separate out the effects of different individual types of adaptation strategies. Missing fine-grained daily weather information for heat-stress modeling
lessons_learned: Past adaptation to climate change successfully reduces current downside risk exposure and diminishes the probability of crop failure, making adapters more resilient to climatic conditions

title: The influence of rainfall and soil-type distribution on uptake of small grains in semi-arid regions of Zimbabwe: A case of Mberengwa and Zvishavane districts
adaptation_focus: Small grain production (sorghum, millet, rapoko) as an alternative to drought-intolerant large grains like maize to build climate change resilience in semi-arid areas
indicators_measured: Small grain uptake rate (percentage of households/wards adopting small grains), precipitation distribution/amount (mm), and spatial distribution of soil types (Ustalfs, Ochrepts, Tropepts)
lessons_learned: Precipitation amount and soil moisture retention capacity have a strong inverse relationship with small grain uptake; areas with the highest rainfall and best clay-heavy soils demonstrate the lowest uptake because maize remains viable

title: Small island nations can achieve food security benefits through climate-adaptive blue food governance by 2050
country_region: Worldwide study covering 31 small island nations
adaptation_focus: Climate-adaptive fisheries governance strategies and adaptive management to handle shifting marine resources and climate change impacts
indicators_measured: Potential seafood surpluses or deficits by 2050 (measured by combining projections of seafood demand and local catch under different future carbon emissions and adaptive management scenarios)
lessons_learned: Adapting fisheries management every 10 years can mitigate the worst-case projections of climate change impacts on locally available seafood for several small island nations, building a seafood surplus by 2050

title: Analysis of farmers’ adaptation to weather extremes in West African Sudan Savanna
adaptation_focus: Farmers' adaptation to recent weather extremes (specifically heat stress, dry spells/drought, and erratic rainfall) along cost-related dimensions (direct vs. supportive measures)
indicators_measured: Number of adaptation strategies, direct/supportive measure adoption propensities, climatic indices, and household socioeconomic characteristics (TLU, farm implements, cropland, credit, extension)
strengths_and_limitations: It is the first study conducted in West Africa to analyze adaptation along cost-related dimensions while simultaneously exploring within- and between-measure interdependencies and estimating joint/marginal probabilities via a multivariate probit and Poisson regression model. It validates subjective farmer perceptions using objective historical grid climate data
lessons_learned: Institutional access to credit and extension services, larger farm sizes, and mechanization are the key drivers that enable smallholder farmers to successfully adopt a complementary mix of low-cost direct and high-cost supportive measures to mitigate the risks of extreme weather events.

title: Farmers’ Perception, Adaptation to Groundwater Salinity, and Climate Change Vulnerability: Insights from North India
adaptation_focus: Farmers' perceptions and adaptation strategies regarding climate change-induced groundwater salinity, water scarcity, and land degradation
process_outcome_domains: knowledge_awareness_learning, decision_making_planning, participation_coproduction , behavioral_change, yields_productivity, access_information_services
indicators_measured: Rainfall trends, maximum-minimum temperatures, potential evapotranspiration (PET), groundwater levels, household demographics (education, gender, social category, family size), landholding size, annual household income, drinking water costs, percent of land degraded, crop yield/productivity, and livestock numbers
strengths_and_limitations: Employs a robust mixed-method approach blending long-term scientific secondary trend data with nuanced primary field insights (survey and case studies) to understand the direct human dimension of an environmental problem
lessons_learned: Perceived impacts act as a far stronger determinant for poor farmers to adapt than general climate change awareness. Farmers perform their own localized cost-benefit analyses before deploying adaptation measures. While economic background heavily dictates how a farmer perceives structural threats (like land degradation vs. crop loss), educational and social backgrounds are the primary drivers determining whether they can successfully implement adaptive technologies (like sprinklers or laser leveling). External institutional, financial, and infrastructural support (such as subsidies or canal access) are vital facilitators to prevent resource-poor communities from falling into permanent poverty traps

title: Social and private profitability of tree-based adaptation options to climate change in a dryland area of Tunisia
adaptation_focus: tree-based adaptation techniques (olive tree plantation and intercropping with cactus) under climate change and water scarcity
process_outcome_domains: knowledge_awareness_learning, decision_making_planning, participation_coproduction , institutional_governance, yields_productivity, behavioral_change
indicators_measured: Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), agricultural land use changes, crop productivity/yield, water demand/costs, and carbon sequestration
strengths_and_limitations: Sets a useful precedent by integrating public, private, and global community cost-benefit perspectives into a quantitative social CBA framework while accounting for local water table externalities
lessons_learned: Rainfed olive tree plantation is completely unprofitable from both private and social perspectives, meaning public subsidies for it are unjustified. Conversely, irrigated olive trees are highly profitable for farmers but generate substantial hidden environmental costs for society via accelerated groundwater decline. Cactus alley-cropping serves as a superior, "win-win" alternative that successfully balances public resource limits with private economic incentives by improving livestock nutrition, lowering feed costs, and tolerating drought without requiring supplemental irrigation

title: Climate change adaptation strategies for smallholder farmers in the Brazilian Sertao.
country_region: Brazil, Sertão (Bahía state, Bacia do Jacuípe county)
adaptation_focus: Small-scale efficient drip irrigation systems to improve food production, and education/extension on balanced animal feeding practices to increase milk and meat productivity
indicators_measured: Meteorological trends (daily minimum/maximum temperatures, annual precipitation, rainfall intensity indices, consecutive dry days); Agricultural/Livestock trends (milk production, number of cows, average milk yield per cow, land cover change); Irrigation pilot metrics (fixed/operating costs, revenues, profit, Net Present Value, Internal Rate of Return, labor hours, returns to labor); Animal feed pilot metrics (milk productivity per animal, value of milk per liter, cost of feed per kg, weight gain of goats, net profit per animal)
strengths_and_limitations: Utilizes a uniquely robust, half-century daily climate dataset to establish micro-climate trends at the municipality level. Features some of the longest, most detailed continuous economic tracking (2–5 years) of smallholder drip irrigation adoption under real-world conditions where farmers actually financed their own systems rather than receiving them as free project handouts. Includes direct, empirically monitored trial data for livestock modifications;The climate trend analysis had to omit some severe drought years in the 1980s and 1990s because local weather stations shut down due to the extreme climate stress, though sensitivity analysis proved this omission did not alter the overall trend significance. The animal feed optimization trials were short-term (1 to 2 months) and featured very small sample sizes (five farmers for dairy, two farmers for goats), which limited the ability to observe long-term impacts on land-use changes or landscape degradation
lessons_learned: the pilot projects demonstrate that smallholder drip irrigation systems drastically improve household income, food consumption, and labor efficiency, yet their success is strictly bound by regional water availability; during severe droughts, ephemeral sources like açudes dry up completely, proving that irrigation cannot stand alone without secure, year-round water storage or regulated groundwater access. Simultaneously, the study shows that simple extension training on optimizing livestock feed ratios—properly balancing roughage, energy, and protein—can rapidly boost average milk yields by 52% and heavily accelerate meat production without forcing farmers into expansive, land-degrading overgrazing practices. Ultimately, to safeguard these agricultural systems against projected climate trends, public policy must pivot away from short-term, reactive emergency relief and instead invest in proactive resilience building during favorable rainy years, focused on scaling water-efficient technologies and optimizing local forage production.

title: Farm women participation in natural resource conservation: technology adoption study in semi-arid regions of India.
country_region: India, Semi-Arid Regions (specifically states of Punjab, Rajasthan, and Gujarat where the studied watersheds are located)
adaptation_focus: Soil and Water Conservation (SWC) technologies within watershed programmes (including mixed cropping, mulching, field bunds, in-situ measures, vegetative barriers, trenching, and contour cultivation) to mitigate land degradation and enhance ecosystem resilience
indicators_measured: Level of SWC technology adoption by farm women; Socio-economic and demographic factors influencing adoption (age, education, farming experience, land holding, risk bearing, social participation, agro-advisory, weather advisory, skill development, ease of use, cost-effectiveness)
strengths_and_limitations: Uses a blended quantitative and qualitative data approach (surveys, FGDs, regression, and correlation modeling) across multiple distinct semi-arid agro-ecosystems to provide a multi-faceted view of gender-focussed technology adoption
lessons_learned: the study reveals that while a majority of farm women (55%) maintain a medium level of Soil and Water Conservation (SWC) technology adoption due to resource constraints and cultural barriers, younger and highly educated women are significantly more receptive to these innovations. Furthermore, active social participation in networks like SHGs or FPOs strongly accelerates adoption by providing critical information channels, whereas long-term farming experience can sometimes create resistance to changing traditional, deeply ingrained practices without targeted awareness campaigns

title: Livestock farmers risk perception and willingness to pay for livestock insurance in flood-prone areas of Punjab.
country_region: Pakistan, Punjab (Dera Ghazi Khan, Layyah, Muzaffargarh, and Rajanpur districts)
adaptation_focus: Willingness to pay for flood and livestock insurance as a financial risk mitigation tool against climate change and extreme flooding events
indicators_measured: Willingness to pay (WTP) for livestock insurance, factors/demographic variables affecting WTP (e.g., household size, farming experience, source of irrigation, availability of animal dispensary, risk perceptions of theft, climate changes, and diseases)
strengths_and_limitations: This study fills a critical academic gap by using face-to-face interviews and the Contingent Valuation Method alongside binary logistic modeling to precisely evaluate the feasibility and determinants of livestock flood insurance among rural survivors in Pakistan
lessons_learned: farmer's willingness to pay for insurance is driven by high risk perceptions, financial literacy, and a lack of local government infrastructure, but ultimately depends on their financial position. Consequently, public subsidies and risk-sharing frameworks are crucial to incentivize private insurers to launch these vital localized disaster programs

title: Evidence-Based Scaling-Up of Evergreen Agriculture for Increasing Crop Productivity, Fodder Supply and Resilience in Tanzania and Malawi
adaptation_focus: Integration of Evergreen Agriculture (fertilizer trees/shrubs, conservation agriculture, and improved soil/water management) to increase ecosystem resilience against climate variability and drought, alongside building capacity for Climate-Smart Agriculture
process_outcome_domains: knowledge_awareness_learning, decision_making_planning, uptake_adoption , participation_coproduction , yields_productivity, risk_reduction
indicators_measured: Socio-economic variables,Biophysical/Soil markers,Land Use/Cover Change metrics,On-farm tree stocking parameters,Energy metrics,USAID indicator No. 14
strengths_and_limitations: Multi-disciplinary partner integration (ICRAF, universities, local research institutes) and successfully co-locating activities with Feed-the-Future action sites;Short project duration, late start of field activities in the growing season, delays in signing contracts, and unexpected changes to the action sites
lessons_learned: Building a common vision and workable program among diverse CG centers and national partners takes longer than expected. Short project timelines complicated by administrative delays in contract signing can severely limit the execution of time-sensitive seasonal field activities (such as establishing tree nurseries or executing experiential exchange visits)

title: Breaking Boundaries: Lower Tail Dependence Can Triple the Economic Value of Index Insurance for Rural Households
Fire risk perpetuates poverty and fire use among Amazonian smallholders
country_region: Brazil, Brazilian Amazon (Eastern Amazon municipalities of Paragominas and Santarém)
adaptation_focus: Smallholder vulnerability, agricultural practices, coordination, and land-use choices under environmental/anthropogenic fire risk feedback loops
process_outcome_domains: knowledge_awareness_learning, decision_making_planning, behavioral_change, access_information_services, livelihoods, resilience_adaptive_capacity
indicators_measured: Farm revenue, per capita per day farm revenue, fire risk (fire hotspot counts via MODIS FIRMS), physical capital (principal component analysis aggregating chemical inputs, machinery, cattle stock, and wire fences), labor days, farm size, environmental features (slope, soil quality, water access), market access (travel time to city), and household characteristics (age, gender, education)
strengths_and_limitations: Combines empirical household survey data with high-resolution remote-sensing fire tracking data; utilizes an instrumental variable GMM approach (Lewbel heteroskedasticity restrictions) to address reverse causality and attenuation bias from detection errors;Analysis only concerns the short run due to lack of longitudinal data; subject to fire measurement errors originating from cloud distortion and detection precision; lacks direct data on farmer fire control investments; assumes risk neutrality; unable to perfectly differentiate between remotely sensed accidental and intended fires
lessons_learned: Individual-focused mitigation policies (like conditional credit exclusions or mechanization subsidies targeted at individual farms) fail because transitioning to fire-free agricultural systems requires landscape-level coordination. A single farm's choice is highly vulnerable to neighbor externalities, creating a low-income fire-poverty trap. To break this lock-in, policy interventions (such as PES or command-and-control mandates) must target whole contiguous communities/settlements simultaneously using a coordinated "Big Push" approach.

title: Do input subsidies promote conservation agriculture? Analyzing Pfumvudza adoption willingness among smallholders in northeastern Zimbabwe using endogenous switching regression
adaptation_focus: Climate-smart conservation agriculture, specifically the Pfumvudza initiative (minimum soil disturbance through planting basins, organic soil mulching, and crop rotation with legumes) to build agricultural resilience against drought and climate change impacts
process_outcome_domains: knowledge_awareness_learning, decision_making_planning, behavioral_change, access_information_services, wellbeing, resilience_adaptive_capacity
indicators_measured: Willingness to adopt the Pfumvudza concept without input subsidy incentives; Access to maize input subsidies; Tropical Livestock Units (TLU); Household perception scores of the Pfumvudza concept; Frequency of extension contact; Tobacco/cotton crop engagement
strengths_and_limitations: It utilizes an endogenous switching probit model to address unobservable confounding factors and eliminate selection bias inherent in non-random subsidy access. It also balances quantitative survey data with qualitative insights from 12 focus group discussions;The study relies strictly on cross-sectional data from a single province (Mashonaland East), limiting its generalizability to areas with different agricultural configurations or tracking how impacts change over time
lessons_learned: Input subsidies alone are insufficient to sustain long-term adoption across all smallholders and may breed dependency. Their effectiveness heavily hinges on land-use context: subsidies significantly improve adoption willingness for communal farmers (who operate smaller, compatible land sizes) but have a negative impact on A1 and old resettlement farmers who manage larger land holdings where manual basin digging has a high labor opportunity cost. Genuine, long-term conservation agriculture practices are driven by increasing extension exposure and cultivating positive farmer perceptions about standalone benefits, rather than relying strictly on external input packages

title: Impact of tank rehabilitation - An analytical study of peri-urban tank of Tamil Nadu
adaptation_focus: Tank rehabilitation as a rainwater harvesting and adaptation strategy against climate change/water scarcity in semi-arid regions
indicators_measured: Internal rate of return (IRR), water availability (mcft), cropping area (ha), crop yield (kg/ha), crop production (quintals), and net cropping benefits (Rs/ha or Rs in lakhs)
strengths_and_limitations: Evaluates multiple structural combinations (63 options) to help policymakers prioritize limited capital based on objective-driven parameters;Confined to a small sampling size of tanks (18 sampled tanks) due to the constraints of time and the cost of data collection
lessons_learned: Periodic tank maintenance is vital to sustain agriculture in semi-arid zones. While individual components like a sluice (Sl) yield the highest financial feasibility (IRR) on their own, integrated strategies like combining sluice, weir, and water spread area rehabilitation ($Sl+W+Wa$) provide the maximum overall benefit to system parameters like absolute water availability, cropping area, production, and net returns. Therefore, the ideal investment option remains highly subjective to the specific policy targets of the governing state
